sentences.text: No.
There was an uncle of mine, the husband of my mother's sister, who was also taken away together with me.
We were putting in a gathering place, in a large-- real large hall.
And the guards there were very, like, lenient.
You could come and go.
They left it so aloof that one could think, well, if nobody's watching you then it's really nothing serious.
So the mentality-- I don't know whether it was done purposely or not-- was such-- look, the windows are open.
You could have gone out through a window.
You could have gone out through the door.
The guard wouldn't even have known whether you are the one who came to visit somebody or you're just leaving.
So maybe there was somebody who left the place and never came back again.
But, since we were kept so loosely, so to say, nobody's came up with the idea-- let me run away.
So we just-- we-- we stayed.
This is all right.
This is our lot, and we have to just conform with that.
And, no-- as far as I know, nobody left the place.
How long were you there?
We were there, I would say-- let's see.
That was in the evening.
Overnight, till the middle of next day.
And my mother came to visit, she brought food, and so forth.
Then they loaded us up.
They had us march to the train station.
And they took us with a regular train-- meaning not a cattle train-- regular train-- they took us on a whole night's ride to a gathering place in Zilina.
That, when we arrived there, we really saw that they are meaning business.
We were there received by [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Hlinka guard, which is the equivalent on the SS guard in Germany.
Black uniforms, wild dogs, rifles, hittings over the head and yelling and screaming, the dogs barking.
They drove into you a fear that you knew this is it.
Now if we had a chance, of course, to turn around, we would've gladly done it.
But that chance was taken away the minute we saw their-- and-- and-- all our-- we were all young people.
I would say maybe my uncle was maybe-- was maybe 35, maybe even younger.
I mean, all these really young people.
We realized right away that things are not good, that this is-- this is-- this is it.
I mean, they were starting beating that we never saw in our life-- really subhuman.
And-- and we knew that something awful is-- is awaiting us, at this point.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The last time I saw my mother, when she came to bring some food to the place, to this first gathering place I mentioned to you.
Well, since we thought we were parting, you know, for a couple of months maybe, you know, she was crying, and we hugged.

And I don't remember exactly the words we said to each other, but it-- it wasn't kind of an emotional good-bye-saying as we would have said if that would have happened in Zilina a day later, because when we know that this is-- they mean business now.
In Zilina, we were there, I think, about 24 hours.
And they gave us some packages of food.
I don't remember what it was.
Of course, it was all non-kosher, so nobody, at such a point, yet had in his wildest dreams that he would eat it.
You were still having plenty, so to say, power in you to withstand such a outrageous-- didn't think to eat non-kosher food.
At that time, they took us, and they loaded us in-- in a cattle train-- packed.
I mean, stuffed.

And, as the hours were going on, we began to realize that-- that these promises that we were given by our leadership at home and-- and by other-- other people, they probably had no knowledge of what's going on in this end of the world.
Otherwise, they probably would have warned us, you know, to escape in the first place.
And they loaded us in the-- in these cattle trains.
And we traveled-- I don't remember how many hours it took them.
It couldn't have been too far, but it probably was between six and 12 hours when we landed in Lublin, in Poland.
The conditions on the trains were, I mean, were awful.
You realized that was, let's say, around Passover-- it was on Passover, 1942.
The weather wasn't very warm.
It was early spring.
And we were squashed there.
You had no way of sitting down.
And there was no way of standing up, either, because one guy was falling upon the other.
So the conditions were horrid.
And people had to go to the bathroom.
If you had to go, you had to go.
It's-- it's-- you know, you have to conform to your human needs.
So needless to say that it was-- it was-- it was awful.
I mean, you-- you began-- That was the-- the-- the beginning of the dehumanization of people.
Where, all of a sudden, you lose your-- your whole dignity.
First of all, just to perform an act of going to the bathroom in front of other people.
I don't have to mention to you what it is.
And then, if it's done en masse, men and women, in the same-- no, excuse me-- there were only men, at this time-- in-- in one place.
It was-- it was an awful, an awful sight, and an awful experience.
It really-- By the time we arrived to Lublin, we like thanked to God to have a little-- little fresh air, there.
And then they gave us these special shoes-- wood shoes.
I forgot-- they have a name for them, but I forgot the name.
They're made in Holland.
What are they called?
Clogs.
This is a shoe in which you can walk-- barely walk-- but you can never run.
So, when they unloaded us in Lublin [INAUDIBLE], in Lublin, they had us put these shoes on, because they were scared somebody will run away.
And they had us march to the Majdanek camp.
I have no recollection of that.
We were just, like, so thankful to have a little fresh air again, to breathe, and to be a little bit free of movement and so, that I-- I can't recall anymore any other thing that sits in my mind.
Yes.
There were SS there.
There were SS and dogs.
But I have to tell you this-- that the reception in Zilina was much worse than the unloading right now.
It was bad, but it wasn't as bad.
I mean, they did a lot of-- that belonged probably to their way of dealing with people, to try to dehumanize you-- yelling, screaming, dogs barking.
It drives into you a fear that, all of a sudden, you don't even have a chance to think, well, wait a minute-- maybe I can escape.
You-- you're so in the midst of fear and subjected to-- to what will happen the next minute that you stay in line and you follow directions.
Yes.
And then marched off to Majdanek.
Marched off to Majdanek.
We walked through the streets.
People were watching us-- the Polish, they were watching.
I mean, they didn't say anything, but it was a pitiful sight, you know, watching people, like-- you have to know, you were slowed down, too, in these, clogs, did you call them?
And it-- it was very hurtful, since you're not used to walking in something.
I don't remember how much the distance was.
And we marched like this until we arrived to the camp, there.
Well, when we arrived in the camp-- we were the first ones.
I can't really recall as to when that Lublin-Majdanek camp opened up.
But all I know is that we were one of the very first ones.
Now, I tried to remember for years what my number was then.
It just really came to my mind-- 3171.
I have tried for years to get to that number.
I really-- it just-- just flew in in my mind.
So I must have been the 3,000th person there, so the person-- the camp could have not been open for too long.
So they marched us in there, of course, took away all our belongings-- the little bit whatever we had.
I don't know-- everybody had a little something.
So the first thing they got-- you got rid of.
They took away your prayer utensils.
They took away your prayer book.
And that's like-- that's like taking away from a human being, today, oxygen.
Because the last thing that was always clinged upon is prayer.
You know, if you have some straw to hold onto-- to faith, to believe in God, to a prayer-- then somehow, if you're a believer, you think you'll survive.
There is somebody there, watching over you.
If they take your prayer book away, and you haven't got that anymore, either, then you're in trouble.
[DRINKS] But many guys were able, somehow, to-- to hide a psalms or a-- or a prayer book or something and smuggle it in in the camp.
And while we were there the first 24 hours, I remember, I already got a hold of a prayer book, somehow, by miracle.
And-- and the first-- the first impression when we came in-- they didn't detail us to work yet.
They left us on the bunks after they give us the numbers, and so forth.
That you had a little time to do or-- whatever you want to.
So we were visiting with each other and trying to find faces that you knew from home, and so forth.
And we were mostly involved in-- in crying and in praying.
The-- the place itself, the barracks we slept in, were like bunks.
I don't recall whether it was two layers, or two tiers, or three tiers.
I was bunking, at that time, still with my uncle.
And my poor uncle-- my heart broke for him, because I was-- he left a wife at home, with two children, and he had a picture.
He had a prayer book.
And he used to cry there all the time and say prayers.
And it really broke my heart, for him more than for myself.
Because it was in the early stages, where-- where the hard work hasn't begun yet, and the big beatings haven't begun yet-- I mean, what we were later experiencing.
So I felt like, I'm good off, in comparison to-- to my uncle.
And that's what we were doing, for a few days.
I cannot recall when they started detailing us to work.
And I also cannot recall what kind of a work I was doing there, specifically.
But I do-- I definitely remember they wouldn't let us sit around there seven weeks doing nothing.
Maybe because of the years, that it just flew out of my mind.
It's-- it's-- I don't remember anymore.
I don't, but, judging by my number, there must have been maybe a little bit less, because some of them must have died or been killed.
They already had there, from what I found out later, some kind of a apparatus to do away with people and to bury them quickly and dispose of them as-- as quick they can.
Because Majdanek was very famous for that.
Of course, it was not known to me, at-- at that time.
No.
All men.
I don't remember.
In Majdanek I was seven weeks.
The food, it was-- it was not enough to live on and too much to die from, so to say.
Again, I cannot recall anymore as far as the portions and the rations that they gave us.
All I remember, it was much better than Auschwitz was, later.
That, I remember.

And you also have to realize that we're talking in a stage where you, little by little, begin losing your vitamins and your power and your strength that you had from previous years, from growing up at home.
So it doesn't take that in-- in seven weeks, you're still capable of-- of eating, so to say, from one self.
If you  had any fat on yourself, it, like, you sustained yourself-- sustained yourself.
The-- the-- I made a friendship there, I remember, in camp, with a guy who used to be a baker, at home.
And he usually was trying to give me a little bit of hope by saying that one day, maybe, he'll get a place in a bakery, and then I'll have enough food.
And it was a dream, of course.
I remember they came out one day with an announcement that they're looking for young people with various trades to be taken to some other destination, unknown.
And they were looking for bakers.
So he-- he-- he adamantly begged me I should also announce myself that I'm a baker and I'll stick with him, and he'll teach me everything about it.

And, sure enough, that was seven weeks later, after the arrival, which was on the Shavuos holiday, on 1942, where we were all lined up and, again, started going back to the train with the same clogs and the same loading up.
And we took that trip to Birkenau.
I told I was a baker.
Except-- except the Germans, in concentration camps, use the same trick that even today the American army is using.
They'll come in, they ask who is a musician, and 15 guys will raise their hands.
And they'll tell them, OK, you're the detail for the latrine cleaning.
I lost-- I lost sight of him, but he never came back.
He did not go with me to Birkenau.
He stayed there, and I never heard of him again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All I know is that I met his wife after the war, and his two children, and he never returned.
The journey to Birkenau were really not as bad as the journey that we made to Lublin.
For some reason, they didn't have us loaded us up in the cattle train as packed as before.
And they did give us some water and some food ration for the road.
And the trip wasn't too long, because from Lublin to-- to Auschwitz it maybe have taken two, three hours, or maybe four hours or so forth.
And they-- I remember going to through several cities, there-- to Katowice, I remember. that was the first time in my life I saw a streetcar, which I never saw at home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we arrived, of course, to Birkenau, the arrival was, I mean, atrocious.
I mean, that-- once the-- the-- the cattle train was opened up, you really saw you came into a living hell.
That was the first time when we-- when we saw Mengele, when-- when dogs were barking there like you thought they're going to tear you apart.
And-- and I have a hard time to find even in English-- in the English language words that will be-- you were tormented for the fear of what's going to happen to you.
You felt like, any second, they're going to-- they're going to shoot you, they're going to kill you, they're going to tear you apart.
And the yelling and the screaming that they created, including with the dogs, were-- were-- were awful.
I mean, it's very hard to describe how, within seconds, you-- you realize, I've lost my identity, I'm a nobody, I'm a nothing, I'm a zero, I'm zilch.
Because, any second, you're dead.
And they took the rifles.

They were shooting, you know, in the air and hitting you with the butt of the rifles, and creating such a chaos that-- that was-- that was-- I mean, it's hard to describe, what it-- what it did to you.
It just-- you-- you fell in line, whether you wanted to or not.
And I-- and I, looking backwards, today, I think that all that thing was like manipulation to try to keep you in a fear in such a manner that you should not get the idea of running away.

In the very early stages, the worst fear of the Germans-- I mean, I've known that from literature that I read, that I know was that, God forbid, the outside world should find out of what's going on in the inside world of concentration camps.
They kept it a secret.
It's-- it-- it was-- they did not want-- don't forget, we're talking about '42-- the world should find out on what they're doing there.
There was a selection there.
And-- and I-- everybody passed.
Because, like I said, we were still in the early stages.
And they took us in to Birkenau.
Because this train was arriving in Birkenau.
All trains arrived-- all transports arrived in Birkenau, not in Auschwitz.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We just-- we just saw-- I mean, I later on found out that Mengele was Mengele.
Didn't know Mengele.
But he was going around and just looking.
And we saw, I would say, quasi able-bodied young people.
We were 1,500 of us, at that time, with this transport.
And the youngest probably was about 16, and the oldest was mostly 35.
So, in seven weeks, even with very little food, we were still able-bodied enough for-- for Germans to use us for whatever they had in mind.
So what happened then?
Then they, again, marched us into the camp-- into the Birkenau camp, and they lined us up for numbering.

And the transports, at that time, were arriving such in a rapid manner, they had a hard time having the people who did the tattooing-- which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] something like-- like you to realize today somebody taking a pen, dipping it in ink, in a bottle, and making each number on the-- on the hand, by hand.
So which was time-consuming.

And they had so many people coming in, from-- from Czechoslovakia-- they were from Slovakia, mostly-- that-- and from Poland-- from all kinds of things-- that they had very hard times to do the numbering at the speed, the rapid speed, that they want to accomplish it.
So somebody came up, just before we arrived there, with an idea to make something the equivalent of a postage stamp which is in a form that you just move a digit and every day you have a new number.
So they made something like this with nu-- with a numerical system.
And you could, with the tip of your pen, just move one letter and get to a higher digit.
And they started tattooing us so that the whole tattoo number came out in one shot.
But they did it to us on the chest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the problem developed later on, because the guys who did it were also, of course-- if I may say so, our own fellows, but guys who were there already.
And they did it with such a rough manner that some of the guys developed some pus and-- and-- and it erased the number.
So they had to re-tattoo them, later on, either on the chest or on the number-- or on the hand.
So mine did not develop any problems, so I'm tattooed on my chest up until today, yet.
How many people, do you think, were tattooed on their chest, altogether?
This was the only transport.
I think they did away with it right after that because they knew they had problems.
So we were 1,500 at that time.
With many of them I kept in touch for quite awhile, in-- while I was in Birkenau and Auschwitz, that had this number.
But by today, I don't think that there are more than five people alive that have a tattooed number on their chest.
And if you want me to name the five, I couldn't.
I couldn't.
After I was tattooed, then they took us to the barracks.
And they assigned us a bunk and a place.
And the next morning, right away, there was a job assignment.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it took a few days.
I started going out to work.
And somehow I started looking around.
Maybe my brothers are in the same place.
Of course, as I mentioned before, I lost contact with them when they were taken away from home.

And, sure enough, you find faces that you still recognize, which was really hard, because Birkenau was-- was, I would say, almost one of the lowest grades of they way they-- they kept the people there.
And-- and sure enough, I found some people that tell me that they saw my brothers there.
And, during the course of work, I kept on going every morning and came back in the evening.
So we tried to run around from one block to the other block, until I found my two brothers there.
Who, in turn, told me that my sister is also someplace in a camp, there, that they found out from-- from somebody.
So we ended up being, now, four of us in the same place.
Of course, we didn't see our sister for a while, till later on we did.
But now, we tried to get so the three brothers together that we all kept in the same block, if we could.
It wasn't so easy.
But at least we tried to go to the same work.
So when one fell down-- fell down, two of them were lifting up the-- the third one who couldn't do it.
Because there, food was, I mean-- it was unavailable.
The-- the-- the-- the segment of the population that the Russians that were captured-- they were really Ukraines-- in the-- in the war.
The Germans were very cunning and smart.
They knew exactly what to do.
They took the people-- Ukraines are-- are Jew-haters, I would say-- on top of list.
They take them, they gave them all the details in Birkenau that have something to do with food.
They put them in charge of the kitchen.
They put them in charge of the latrines.
They put them in every kind of a place where they could do with the Jewish population whatever they wanted to.
And, of course, they would come in the morning, bring you the coffee-- which was supposed to be coffee or tea-- in-- in a barrel, in an open barrel.
And the Block Alteste, the guy who was in charge on the barracks, if it wasn't enough what they did with us, would put that barrel down.
You'd have to line up there to get a cup and your daily ration of bread.

And if one guy or another didn't like the way we were moving, or the way we were standing, or the way we were receiving our-- our food, he took the whole barrel, emptied it on the floor, and that was the end of it.

And-- and most of us, because we were really starving for a little moisture-- we were completely dehydrated-- used to lie down on the ground to, like, sip the-- the-- the moisture off the ground, from the mud, just to get a little fluid in your body.
So the-- the human-- the dehumanizing of-- of the population there was to such an extent that-- that the worst they can perform, the more-- the Germans didn't even have to do the job.
All the Germans did during the day-- they used to come in and just count us, in the morning, as we marched out from the camps, and in the evening, as we came back to the camps.
And all they were interested in is the body count was correct.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the daily sight was that, if somebody died at work-- which was very common-- or somebody died on the road because he was beaten up by anybody who could just raise his hand and wanted to do so-- all they were interested, that all the dead bodies were accounted for.

So you used to come back from-- from your place of work, and-- and you used to have to drag these bodies-- the ones-- we were able, still, to walk, had to drag the dead bodies with us, to bring them in into main camp.
I don't know  if you realize that Birkenau had what they called 'eine kleine Postenkette' and a large Postenkette.
Meaning that, in the night, the camp was, to an extent, only around the barracks, with electric wires and towers with SS around the camp.
That was called the 'kleine Postenkette'-- the small circle around that they had to watch.
During the day, that expanded to such an extent that it was miles.
Because every detail had to go out of the camp to work.
So that become, all of a sudden, a much larger camp than it was before.
